words.g: the a prophet or inspired man

verses.flow: In the second year of Darius the king, in the sixth month, on the first day of the month, the word of YHWH came by the hand of Haggai the prophet unto Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, and unto Joshua the son of Jehozadak, the high priest, saying,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The date "second year of Darius" fixes this oracle precisely to August 29, 520 BC, making Haggai among the most precisely datable texts in all Scripture. Darius I (Hystaspes) had recently consolidated Persian power after the upheavals following Cambyses' death. Zerubbabel as "governor" (peha) reflects Judah's status as a Persian sub-province (Yehud), while the presence of a high priest alongside a Davidic governor reflects the dual leadership structure of the restoration community. (Ezra 3:2; Zech 4:6-14)

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH of hosts (tseva'ot): This people say, The time has not come, the time for the house of YHWH to be built.
verses.note: HEBREW: "YHWH of hosts" translates YHWH tseva'ot (Strong's H6635), a divine title appearing over 250 times in the OT and concentrated heavily in the prophets. Tseva'ot ("armies" or "hosts") likely refers to the divine council and celestial armies over which YHWH presides as sovereign. The people's rationalization — "the time has not come" — reflects spiritual torpor cloaked in pragmatic language, a pattern the prophets consistently confront. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The title YHWH tseva'ot invokes YHWH's role as commander of heavenly armies, situating the rebuilding of the Temple within a cosmic, not merely political, frame. (Ps 82:1; Isa 6:3; 1 Kgs 22:19)

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

words.g: the a prophet or inspired man

verses.flow: Then the word of YHWH came by the hand of Haggai the prophet, saying,
Is it a time for you yourselves to dwell in your paneled houses, while this house lies in ruins?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The word "paneled" (sefunim, from saphan, Strong's H5603) refers to costly cedar-lined interiors, the same material used in Solomon's Temple (1 Kgs 6:9). The contrast is devastating: the people lavish on their own dwellings the very resources appropriate to YHWH's house. This verse exposes a disordered theology of worship in which human comfort is prioritized over divine habitation. Covenantally, the Temple was the locus of YHWH's glory-presence (shekinah) among his people; its ruin signals a broken covenant relationship. (Ps 132:3-5; 2 Sam 7:2)

verses.flow: Now therefore thus says YHWH of hosts: Set your hearts upon your ways.

words.m: conjunction + particle + verb hithpael participle active

verses.flow: You have sown much, and brought in little; you eat, but you have not enough; you drink, but you are not filled with drink; you clothe yourselves, but no one is warm; and he who earns wages does so to put them into a bag with holes.
Thus says YHWH of hosts: Set your hearts upon your ways.

verses.flow: Go up to the hill country and bring wood and build the house, that I may take pleasure in it and that I may be glorified (kavod), says YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: "That I may be glorified" renders the Niphal of kavad (Strong's H3513), meaning to be honored, weighty, or glorified. Kavod ("glory") is the same root as the visible manifestation of YHWH's presence that filled the Tabernacle (Exod 40:34) and Solomon's Temple (1 Kgs 8:11). TYPOLOGY: The call to build a house for YHWH's glory anticipates the NT revelation that YHWH's glory ultimately dwelt not in a stone edifice but in the incarnate Son (John 1:14, "the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, and we beheld his glory"). The rebuilt Temple is thus a typological waystation pointing to the true Temple. (John 2:19-21; Rev 21:22)

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil perfect 2nd masc plur
conjunction + verb qal perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: You looked for much, and behold, it came to little; and when you brought it home, I blew it away. Why? declares YHWH of hosts. Because of my house that lies in ruins, while each of you runs to his own house.
Therefore the heavens above you have withheld the dew, and the earth has withheld its produce.

verses.flow: And I have called for a drought upon the land and upon the hills, upon the grain and upon the new wine and upon the oil, and upon what the ground brings forth, and upon man and upon beast, and upon all the labor of their hands.

words.g: the a prophet or inspired man

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: Then Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel, and Joshua the son of Jehozadak, the high priest, and all the remnant of the people, obeyed the voice of YHWH their God, and the words of Haggai the prophet, as YHWH their God had sent him; and the people feared before the face of YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The threefold response — obedience of leaders, obedience of the remnant, and fear before YHWH — mirrors the covenant renewal pattern found throughout Deuteronomy and the Former Prophets. The word "remnant" (she'erit, Strong's H7611) is a theologically loaded term, evoking Isaiah's and Micah's theology of a faithful surviving core through whom YHWH would fulfill his promises. The people's fear (yare', Strong's H3372) before YHWH is not panic but the covenant reverence that is the beginning of wisdom (Prov 1:7). (Isa 10:20-22; Mic 5:7-8; Ezra 5:1-2)

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then Haggai, the messenger of YHWH, spoke the message of YHWH to the people, saying, I am with you, declares YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: Haggai is called "the messenger of YHWH" (mal'ak YHWH, Strong's H4397), the same term used for angelic messengers and theophanies throughout the OT. This is the only place in the Hebrew prophets where a prophet is explicitly designated by this title, blurring the boundary between prophetic and angelic function and underscoring the divine authority of his message. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The mal'ak YHWH language situates the prophet within the divine council's communication structure — a heavenly herald bearing the King's decree to earth. (Isa 6:8; Mal 3:1; Rev 1:1)

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: And YHWH stirred up the spirit (ruach) of Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, and the spirit (ruach) of Joshua the son of Jehozadak, the high priest, and the spirit (ruach) of all the remnant of the people; and they came and worked on the house of YHWH of hosts, their God,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "YHWH stirred up the spirit (ruach)" employs the Piel of 'ur (Strong's H5782), meaning to rouse, awaken, or incite. This divine awakening of the ruach (spirit, Strong's H7307) of both civil and religious leaders demonstrates that the restoration project is not merely a human political achievement but a sovereign act of YHWH. TYPOLOGY: The stirring of Zerubbabel's ruach prefigures the eschatological outpouring of the Spirit upon God's people (Joel 2:28-29; Acts 2), and Zerubbabel himself is a Davidic type pointing toward the greater Son of David who would build the true Temple of God. (Zech 4:6-9; Matt 1:12-13; Eph 2:21-22)

verses.flow: on the twenty-fourth day of the sixth month, in the second year of Darius the king.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The date "twenty-fourth day of the sixth month" (approximately September 21, 520 BC) indicates that work on the Temple resumed only twenty-three days after Haggai's initial oracle. This rapid response testifies to the effectiveness of prophetic preaching when accompanied by the sovereign work of YHWH's Spirit (v. 14). The precision of dating throughout Haggai (cf. 2:1, 10, 20) reflects both scribal care and the prophet's conviction that YHWH acts concretely within historical time. (Ezra 5:1-2; Zech 1:1)

words.g: the a prophet or inspired man

verses.flow: In the seventh month, on the twenty-first day of the month, the word of YHWH came by the hand of Haggai the prophet, saying,
Speak now to Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, and to Joshua the son of Jehozadak, the high priest, and to the remnant of the people, saying,
Who is left among you who saw this house in its former glory (kabod)? And how do you see it now? Is it not as nothing in your eyes?
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The question "who saw this house in its former glory?" implies that elderly returnees (cf. Ezra 3:12) could recall Solomon's temple destroyed in 586 BC, some 66 years prior. The contrast between past splendor and present modest foundations produced public mourning. This rhetorical question sets up YHWH's counter-promise in vv. 6-9.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: Yet now be strong (chazaq), O Zerubbabel — declares YHWH — and be strong, O Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest, and be strong, all you people of the land — declares YHWH — and work, for I am with you — declares YHWH of hosts (Sabaoth).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Chazaq" (Strong's H2388) — "be strong, take courage" — appears three times in rapid succession, once for each addressee (governor, priest, people), forming a triple charge mirroring Joshua's commissioning (Josh 1:6-9). The phrase "I am with you" (ani ittkem) is the foundational covenant assurance echoing the Immanuel motif.

words.m: preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: According to the word that I cut with you when you came out of Egypt, and my Spirit (ruach) remains in your midst — do not fear.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "according to the word I cut (karath) with you" recalls the Mosaic covenant; the verb karath ("to cut") is the standard covenant-making term, alluding to the sacrificial rite of covenant ratification. The continuing presence of the ruach (Spirit) in the community's midst anticipates the new covenant promise of the Spirit's indwelling (Ezek 36:27; Joel 2:28).

words.g: the a sea or large body of water

verses.flow: For thus says YHWH of hosts: Yet once more, in a little while, I will shake the heavens and the earth and the sea and the dry land.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The cosmic shaking of heavens, earth, sea, and dry land echoes theophanic language of Sinai (Exod 19:18; Ps 68:8) and anticipates eschatological judgment. The writer of Hebrews (12:26-27) cites this verse explicitly, interpreting the shaking as the removal of the old order to establish the unshakable kingdom of the Messiah.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil perfect 1st common sing
conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd common plur

words.g: and to fill or be full of
words.m: conjunction + verb piel perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: And I will shake all the nations, and the treasure (chemdah) of all the nations shall come, and I will fill this house with glory (kabod) — declares YHWH of hosts.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Chemdah" (Strong's H2532) — "desire, treasure, precious thing" — is rendered in the singular collective. Some traditions (following the Vulgate's "Desideratus cunctis gentibus") interpreted this as a messianic title ("the Desired of all nations"), though the Hebrew verb "shall come" is plural, suggesting nations rather than a person. Nonetheless, the passage was broadly received as anticipating the Messiah's advent filling the Second Temple with his presence (Mal 3:1; Luke 2:22-38). TYPOLOGY: Jesus' presentation and ministry at the Second Temple (Luke 2; John 2) fulfills the promise that YHWH's glory would fill this very structure, greater than the Shekinah of the Solomonic house.

verses.flow: The silver is mine, and the gold is mine — declares YHWH of hosts.
The latter glory (kabod) of this house shall be greater than the former — declares YHWH of hosts — and in this place I will give peace (shalom) — declares YHWH of hosts.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "latter glory" (kabod ha-acharon) greater than the former is the theological apex of the book. The shalom YHWH promises "in this place" is not merely the cessation of conflict but the fullness of covenantal well-being. TYPOLOGY: Christian interpretation identifies the fulfillment in the incarnate Word entering the Second Temple precincts (John 2:13-22; Matt 21:12-16), bringing the true Shekinah in flesh (John 1:14), and ultimately establishing eschatological shalom through his atoning work.

words.g: the a prophet or inspired man

verses.flow: On the twenty-fourth day of the ninth month, in the second year of Darius, the word of YHWH came by the hand of Haggai the prophet, saying,
Thus says YHWH of hosts: Ask now the priests for a ruling (torah),
verses.note: HEBREW: "Torah" here is used in the technical priestly sense of "ruling" or "instruction" (Strong's H8451), not simply "law." The priests served as halakhic arbiters in cultic matters (Deut 17:8-13; Lev 10:10-11). The use of a priestly torah-inquiry as a parabolic device is notable rhetorical strategy.

verses.flow: If someone carries holy meat in the fold of his garment and touches with his fold bread, or stew, or wine, or oil, or any food, does it become holy? The priests answered and said, No.

verses.flow: Then Haggai said, If someone who is unclean by contact with a corpse touches any of these, does it become unclean? The priests answered and said, It becomes unclean.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then Haggai answered and said, So is this people, and so is this nation before me — declares YHWH — and so is every work of their hands, and what they offer there is unclean.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH declares the people and their offerings ritually unclean because unholiness communicates more readily than holiness — a principle illustrated by the priestly ruling of vv. 12-13. This indicts not the rebuilding effort itself but the prior years of self-focused neglect (cf. Hag 1:4-6). The corruption of worship taints the entire agricultural economy, reversing covenant blessing.

verses.flow: And now, consider from this day onward. Before stone was placed upon stone in the temple (hekal) of YHWH,

verses.flow: from that time when one came to a heap of twenty, there were but ten; when one came to the wine vat to draw out fifty from the press, there were but twenty.
I struck you with blight (shiddaphon) and with mildew and with hail in all the works of your hands, yet you did not turn to me — declares YHWH.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The agricultural curses of blight (shiddaphon), mildew (yerakon), and hail recall the covenant curse-lists of Deuteronomy (28:22) and parallel ANE treaty-curse formulae in which the suzerain deity withdraws blessing from a disloyal vassal. The indictment "you did not turn to me" (ve-lo-shavtem aday) echoes the prophetic refrain in Amos 4:6-11, likely a deliberate intertextual link.

verses.flow: Consider now from this day onward, from the twenty-fourth day of the ninth month. From the day that the foundation of the temple (hekal) of YHWH was laid, consider:
Is the seed yet in the barn? And as for the vine, the fig tree, the pomegranate, and the olive tree — they have not yet borne. But from this day I will bless.

verses.flow: The word of YHWH came a second time to Haggai on the twenty-fourth day of the month, saying,
Speak to Zerubbabel, governor of Judah, saying, I am about to shake the heavens and the earth,

verses.flow: and I will overturn the throne of kingdoms and destroy the strength of the kingdoms of the nations, and I will overturn the chariot and its riders; and the horses and their riders shall go down, every one by the sword of his brother.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The overthrowing of thrones and kingdoms employs cosmic-warfare language resonant with divine council theology. The "throne of kingdoms" represents both earthly imperial powers and, in Second Temple cosmology, the heavenly patrons (sons of God) who stand behind them (cf. Deut 32:8; Dan 10:13, 20). YHWH's shaking removes both the human structures and their supernatural backing, establishing his own universal sovereignty.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 1st common sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: On that day — declares YHWH of hosts — I will take you, O Zerubbabel my servant, son of Shealtiel — declares YHWH — and I will make you like a signet ring (chotam), for I have chosen you — declares YHWH of hosts.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The signet ring (chotam, Strong's H2368) is the image of intimate authority and royal identity — the instrument by which a king authenticates decrees. Significantly, Zerubbabel's ancestor Jeconiah (Coniah) was declared by YHWH to be "torn off" like a signet ring (Jer 22:24), a curse now reversed in his grandson. This reversal is christologically loaded: Matthew's genealogy (1:12) traces the Davidic line through Zerubbabel to Jesus, in whom the signet-ring promise is ultimately fulfilled — the one who bears the Father's full authority and image (Heb 1:3; John 14:9). THEOLOGICAL: The closing "I have chosen you" (ki-vekha bacharti) is election language of the highest order, recalling the Davidic covenant (Ps 89:3-4) and pointing forward to the Chosen Servant of Isaiah (42:1), whose identity converges in the Messiah.
